assertion.text: Erdős Problem #489 remains OPEN. Statement: Let $A\subseteq \mathbb{N}$ be a set such that $\lvert A\cap [1,x]\rvert=o(x^{1/2})$. Let $B=\{ n\geq 1 : a\nmid n\textrm{ for all }a\in A\}$. If $B=\{b_1 < b_2 < \cdots\}$ then is it true that $$\lim_{x \to \infty} \frac{1}{x}\sum_{b_i < x}(b_{i+1}-b_i)^2$$ exists (and is finite)? For example, when $A=\{p^2: p\textrm{ prime}\}$ then $B$ is the set of squarefree numbers, and the existence of this limit was proved by Erdős. See also [208]. Topics: number theory. Erdős prize: no. Statement is machine-verified in Lean (formal-conjectures). OEIS: N/A.
